Therefore, the ranking from most stable to least stable is Heights, Oak Ridge, West End, and Riverdale.

Socioeconomic disparities and racial inequality also play significant roles in creating and exacerbating the disparities observed. This combination means that systemic racism intersects with poverty and social disadvantage to disproportionately impact marginalized communities.

The overwhelming consensus among most contemporary scientists is that environmental and societal factors play a much larger role than genetics in observed group differences.

I understand you're asking about the philosophical arguments in professional debates about meritocracy. To counter this, you can focus on how equity seeks to address systemic barriers that prevent pure meritocracy from functioning in the real world.

The term "primitive" is a very loaded one, and it's not just about being "outdated." It's a label that's been used to dismiss and belittle entire groups of people. When people talk about "primitive" societies, they're often referring to cultures that have been historically marginalized and are still struggling to adapt to modern, industrialized life. These groups are often seen as lacking the sophistication and advancement of Western civilization. It's important to recognize that these cultures have their own unique systems and ways of life, which are often misunderstood by those who are more accustomed to modern societal structures.

They'll argue that the "gap" you see is simply because Western society is obsessed with its own brand of industrial, bureaucratic complexity, and that's the only kind of organization that matters. In reality, they're just using different, often less "advanced" methods that don't measure up to our standards.
